title: CO-50 Insurance Denial: What It Means and How to Appeal
meta_description: CO-50 means your insurer determined a service was not medically necessary. Learn exactly what to do, what to include in your appeal, and how to win.
target_problem: Patient received a CO-50 denial (not medically necessary) and needs to appeal.
body:
A CO-50 denial means your insurance company determined the service was not medically necessary under your plan's criteria.

Fewer than 1% of patients appeal denials, even though 40–60% of those who do win.

## What CO-50 Means

CO-50 is a Claim Adjustment Reason Code (CARC) used by insurers to indicate that a service was denied because the plan does not consider it medically necessary for your diagnosis or clinical situation.

## How to Appeal a CO-50 Denial

1. Request the insurer's medical necessity criteria in writing — they are required to provide this under federal law.
2. Ask your doctor to write a Letter of Medical Necessity documenting the clinical rationale.
3. Submit a formal internal appeal within your plan's deadline (typically 180 days from the denial date).
4. If the internal appeal fails, request an Independent Medical Review — a third party must review the decision.

## Disclaimer

This content does not constitute legal or medical advice. MyAdvocate is not a law firm. For questions specific to your situation, consult a qualified attorney or healthcare professional.
